Palpitations — symptom
Ventricular arrhythmias are more likely in patients with preexisting coronary artery disease or risk factors for CAD as a cause for palpitations.
Palpitations caused by sustained tachyarrhythmias in patients with CAD can be accompanied by angina pectoris or dyspnea due to increased left atrial and pulmonary venous pressure.
Premature atrial or ventricular contractions are commonly associated with intermittent palpitations, particularly if the post-extrasystolic beat is sensed by the patient.
Muscle weakness — symptom
Myasthenia gravis is likely causing intermittent muscle weakness due to autoimmune neuromuscular junction dysfunction.
Pathologic fatigability with abnormal clinical or laboratory findings suggests a primary muscle disease such as myophosphorylase deficiency.
Electromyography and creatine kinase levels are elevated in patients with polymyositis, indicating muscle inflammation and damage.
MRI Brain: Acute ischemic changes in watershed regions of anterior, middle, and posterior cerebral arteries — imaging
The anterior cerebral artery territory infarction may be more extensive than initially suggested by diffusion-weighted imaging.
The patient's watershed region ischemic changes on MRI are likely due to severe stenosis of the left internal carotid artery.
A penumbral-ischemic region is present in the posterior cerebral artery territory, indicating potential for salvageable tissue.
A penumbral-ischemic region is present in the posterior cerebral artery territory, indicating potential for salvageable tissue.
A proximal posterior cerebral artery occlusion may have occurred due to embolic causes, as suggested by the higher proportion of presumed embolic occlusions in our experience.
The infarct location and extent will be determined by the site of the occlusion and the arrangement of the circle of Willis, potentially sparing some areas within the PCA territory.

Reversal of ischemia may salvage penumbral tissue, implying that prompt treatment of secondary brain insults such as systemic hypotension or fever is crucial to prevent exacerbation of the primary injury.

Severe stenosis of the left internal carotid artery (arrow), just above the common carotid artery bifurcation, may indicate increased risk for ACA territory infarction on MRI.
Axial diffusion-weighted MRI showing multifocal acute infarctions in the left ACA-MCA arterial border zone (watershed) suggests a possible watershed infarct due to compromised blood flow.

Chief complaint: weakness in both arms and legs — symptom
Hemiplegia-induced shoulder-hand syndrome may cause weakness in both arms and legs due to vasomotor changes and secondary arthropathy.
Frozen shoulder or periarthritis may develop as a complication of hemiplegia, leading to weakness in the affected limbs.
Thyrotoxic myopathy, a complication of hyperthyroidism, may present with progressive weakness and wasting of skeletal musculature.
CT perfusion scans with delayed or incomplete contrast enhancement may predict a larger infarct size compared to diffusion-weighted imaging.
CT perfusion defects may indicate the ischemic penumbra and predict infarct size more accurately than diffusion-weighted imaging alone.
Perfusion MRI can differentiate between viable tissue at risk of infarction and irreversibly damaged tissue based on mean transit time and cerebral blood volume.
Delayed or incomplete contrast enhancement in CT perfusion scans may be associated with larger infarct sizes due to reduced collateral flow.
